title: Content-Type & Accept Headers
overview: Understand content negotiation using Content-Type and Accept headers.
whyItMatters: APIs can support multiple data formats (JSON, XML, HTML). Content negotiation ensures the client and server understand what format is being sent and what format is expected in return.
explanation: The `Content-Type` header tells the receiver what format the body of the message is in. For example, a POST request with `Content-Type: application/json` tells the server to parse the body as JSON. The `Accept` header tells the server what format the client wants the response to be in. If a client sends `Accept: application/xml`, the server should serialize the response to XML (or return 406 Not Acceptable if it cannot).
keyPoints: `Content-Type` = format of the payload being sent.
`Accept` = format desired for the response payload.
Servers use these headers for media-type negotiation.
In REST Assured, use `.contentType()` and `.accept()` shortcuts.
codeExample:
given().
    contentType(ContentType.JSON). // Tells server: I'm sending JSON
    accept(ContentType.XML).       // Tells server: Please reply with XML
    body(userJson).
when().
    post("/users").
then().
    statusCode(201).
    contentType(ContentType.XML);  // Asserts the server replied with XML
realWorldExample: An enterprise API allows external partners to download data in either JSON or CSV format. Your test suite sends two requests: one with `Accept: application/json` (asserting the response parses as JSON) and one with `Accept: text/csv` (asserting the response parses as CSV).
commonMistakes: Setting the `Accept` header to JSON but using `XmlPath` to parse the response.
Forgetting the `Content-Type` on a POST request, causing the server to reject the payload with a 415 Unsupported Media Type error.
Confusing the two headers (e.g., trying to use `Accept` to declare the request body's format).
bestPractices: Always specify `Content-Type` for requests with bodies (POST, PUT, PATCH).
Always specify `Accept` to prevent the server from falling back to an unexpected default format.
Write tests that verify the server returns a 415 when an unsupported `Content-Type` is sent, and a 406 when an unsupported `Accept` is sent.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the difference between the Content-Type and Accept headers?
interviewQuestions.a: Content-Type specifies the format of the data being sent in the current HTTP message body. Accept specifies the format the client expects the server to use for the response body.

interviewQuestions.q: What HTTP status code should a server return if it doesn't support the format requested in the Accept header?

revisionSummary: Content-Type describes the request body format; Accept requests the response format.
